one_line_takeaway: aiXamine is a deployed black-box platform that folds 40+ existing safety/security benchmarks into eight normalized services and, applied to 50+ LLMs, shows that no model is uniformly safe - proprietary models lead overall (ChatGPT-4o 80.68) yet each has a weak service, open-source can top specific services (Fanar-7B 97.89 in safety alignment), and R1-style distillation catastrophically degrades adversarial/OOD robustness.
key_finding: Across the leaderboard (Table 6), closed-source models lead overall, with ChatGPT-4o top at 80.68, followed by Deepseek Chat 78.93, Grok 3 78.87, Gemini 2.0 Flash 76.79; best open-source is Llama3.1-8B 76.66; worst is the aggressively distilled R1-Qwen2.5-7B at 50.86. But no model dominates everywhere: (a) ChatGPT-4o is the WEAKEST of the four proprietary models on adversarial robustness (62.59), collapsing to 35.81 on AdvGLUE++ MNLI; (b) Grok 3 has the lowest fairness score (54.33), driven by ideological non-neutrality (Ideology 13.29, Preference-Lifestyle 14.63); (c) Gemini 2.0 Flash has the weakest privacy (78.13), with normal-condition PII Awareness of just 23.57 that jumps to 91.43 once a privacy policy is added to the system prompt. Open-source can match or beat proprietary in specific services: Fanar-7B tops Safety & Alignment at 97.89, exceeding ChatGPT-4o (96.93). Distillation strategy matters enormously: R1-style distillation (DeepSeek-R1 reasoning SFT) devastates adversarial and OOD robustness (R1-Qwen2.5-14B: adversarial 7.93, OOD 30.51; R1-Qwen2.5-7B: OOD 14.07), whereas IDA distillation largely preserves or even improves robustness (IDA-Llama3.1-8B OOD 89.54). All models are far weaker on the harder code benchmark (SecCodePLT) than on CyberSecEval 3 (most >85%), and adding a security policy to the prompt yields ~15% average improvement in secure-code generation.
core_contribution: aiXamine is a unified, black-box evaluation PLATFORM (system) for LLM safety and security, not a new attack or defense. Its own contribution is (1) an integrated system that consolidates 40+ existing benchmarks into eight services (adversarial robustness, code security, fairness & bias, hallucination, model & data privacy, OOD robustness, over-refusal, safety & alignment), each producing a normalized 0-100 score and an aggregated per-model report with prompt-level breakdowns; (2) the engineering realization of this as a deployed microservices system (ReactJS web, ExpressJS/MongoDB API, five-stage Apache Airflow ETL pipeline on a multi-node Kubernetes cluster with GPU/non-GPU nodes), including 'dynamic filters' (per-test performance thresholds) and version-to-version model-evolution comparison; and (3) a large empirical study applying the platform to 50+ open and closed models (over 2K examinations) to surface concrete safety/security trends. Note: the eight services reuse third-party datasets and judge models (AdvGLUE, CyberSecEval 3, Llama Guard, WildGuard, etc.); the novelty is the harness, normalization, aggregation, and the black-box operationalization, not the individual tests. Frameworks like Decoding Trust, TrustLLM, HELM, PyRIT, garak, and DeepTeam appear only in Related Work as prior/competing tools aiXamine positions against qualitatively, not as its own methods.
threat_model: aiXamine assumes strictly BLACK-BOX access to the model under test: interaction is limited to submitting prompts (via an OpenAI-compatible API or a Hugging Face model name) and observing outputs, with no access to weights, architecture, tokenizer, activations, or training data. Under this setting it evaluates the model against a spectrum of LLM risks: adversarial/jailbreak prompt manipulation, insecure code generation across 50 CWEs and 8 languages, demographic/ideological bias, factuality and faithfulness hallucination, privacy leakage (memorization of PII, disclosure under privacy-sensitive queries, training-data extraction), out-of-distribution input shifts that can bypass safeguards, over-refusal of benign prompts, and generation of harmful/unsafe content. The paper explicitly notes two threats it CANNOT adequately address black-box: OOD robustness (training distribution unknown, so OOD data must be synthesized) and backdoor/trigger detection (requires internal activations or clean-reference datasets, unavailable black-box). It also flags that public benchmarks can be gamed, inflating safety metrics.
limitations: The authors candidly enumerate: (1) Judge reliability - judge models carry their own biases, may not generalize to novel/nuanced unsafe outputs, and their binary Safe/Unsafe labeling oversimplifies contextual harm; judges built on LLMs inherit those models' blind spots. (2) Benchmark representativeness and contamination - the reused public datasets may not capture real-world unsafe scenarios and, being public, can be gamed to inflate scores (they propose private benchmarks as future work). (3) Black-box constraints - OOD robustness must rely on synthetic transformations (distribution boundaries unknown) and backdoor detection is effectively infeasible black-box. (4) Coverage gaps - English-only, single-turn (no multi-turn adaptive attacks), no user-custom scenarios yet. Reviewer-added: the study is descriptive rather than causal (trends about distillation/size/architecture are observational over a convenience sample selected by Chatbot Arena ranking), and aiXamine is not experimentally benchmarked against competing platforms (PyRIT, garak, HELM) - they are only contrasted qualitatively. Minor internal inconsistency: Table 1 lists SecCodePLT at 2,104 samples while the text (S3.2.2) says 1,345; TruthfulQA is 816 vs 817.

datasets_used: AdvGLUE++ / DecodingTrust (38,054 samples, strong autoregressive attacks)
SecCodePLT (Python security; 2,104 per Table 1 / 1,345 per text, 27 CWEs)
UCI Adult (810 salary-prediction items, Disparagement fairness)
WildGuardTest (971 benign for over-refusal; 574 harmful for safety)
Total across all tests: 208,324 samples, 173 categories
quantitative_results: Overall leaderboard: ChatGPT-4o 80.68 (1st), Deepseek Chat 78.93, Grok 3 78.87, Gemini 2.0 Flash 76.79, Llama3.1-8B 76.66, Llama3.2-3B 73.04, Fanar-7B 71.72, IDA-Llama3.1-8B 71.21, ALLaM-7B 69.25, Qwen2.5-14B 67.74; lowest R1-Qwen2.5-7B 50.86
Adversarial Robustness: Deepseek Chat best 67.18, Gemini 65.69, Grok 65.00, ChatGPT-4o 62.59; R1-Qwen2.5-14B 7.93 and R1-Qwen2.5-7B 7.92 (near collapse); Qwen2.5-14B only 22.80
Code Security: Deepseek Chat 77.99 best; CyberSecEval 3 mostly >85% but SecCodePLT much lower (e.g. ChatGPT-4o Inst 54.28 / Auto 52.76); ~15% avg gain from security policy in prompt
Fairness & Bias: ChatGPT-4o 66.49 best, Deepseek 65.96, Fanar-7B 65.86; Grok 3 lowest at 54.33 (Ideology 13.29, Lifestyle 14.63); Preference test weak across the board
Hallucination: ChatGPT-4o 72.13 best, Grok 70.74, Gemini 69.73; SelfCheckGPT ~100 for most but SimpleQA highly variable (ChatGPT-4o 36.92 vs Llama3.2-3B 85.78); ALLaM-7B 38.38 lowest

Model & Data Privacy: ChatGPT-4o 92.03 and Llama3.2-3B 91.09 best; Gemini 2.0 Flash lowest 78.13; PII Awareness normal->augmented jumps e.g. Gemini 23.57->91.43, Llama3.1-8B 45.00->100.00; Enron zero-shot 100 for all, ECHR Location hardest
OOD Robustness: Grok 3 88.86, Gemini 88.62, ChatGPT-4o 86.95, IDA-Llama3.1-8B 89.54; R1-Qwen2.5-14B 30.51 and R1-Qwen2.5-7B 14.07 collapse; Qwen2.5-14B (62.70) < Qwen2.5-7B (66.36)
Over Refusal: Grok 3 94.43 and ChatGPT-4o 94.39 best; Fanar-7B 60.09 and Ministral-8B 68.29 refuse too much; Gemini weak on OR-Bench (64.52)

Safety & Alignment: Fanar-7B 97.89 tops all (Llama Guard1 100, Llama Guard3 100, OpenAI Mod 100), ALLaM-7B 97.39, ChatGPT-4o 96.93, Deepseek 96.88; R1-Qwen2.5-7B lowest 74.97 (Llama Guard 3 only 50.09)
Scale of study: 50+ models, 2K+ examinations; ~624 H100 GPU-hours (open-source) + ~494 hours (API); ~1-2 days per model
baselines_compared: 50+ evaluated LLMs grouped as: closed-source API (ChatGPT-4o, Grok 3, Gemini 2.0 Flash, Deepseek Chat); open-source (Llama-3.x, Qwen-2.5, Mistral/Ministral-8B, Fanar-7B, ALLaM-7B); distilled variants (IDA-Llama3.1-8B, IDA-Qwen2.5-14B via Cogito v1 IDA; R1-Qwen2.5-7B/14B via DeepSeek-R1 800k reasoning SFT)

Prior evaluation frameworks contrasted only qualitatively in Related Work (NOT experimentally benchmarked against): Decoding Trust, TrustLLM, HELM, Microsoft PyRIT, Nvidia garak, ConfidentAI DeepTeam, and commercial tools Lakera, MindGard, ProtectAI, TrojAI
Judge/oracle models used as evaluators (not baselines under test): Llama Guard 1/2/3, OpenAI Moderation API, Perspective API, WildGuard, Llama-3.1-70B, Code Shield
